Vibhaa Sharma

Bachelor of Engineering (Honours) in Engineering Science

Testing & Systems Engineering — Auckland Programme for Space Systems

Assembling a test and flight electron emitter PCB

What: Assembled both test and flight versions of an electron emitter payload for an aerospace project.

How: Followed detailed assembly documentation, performed soldering and inspections, and used test jigs and verification equipment to validate functionality.

Results: Produced reliable flight-ready hardware while gaining practical experience in electronics assembly, testing, and quality assurance processes.

Integration testing method and documentation of a radiation payload with cobalt-60

What: Developed integration test documentation for a radiation-detection payload.

How: Translated system requirements into structured test procedures and worked with the software team to define test cases and verification methods for testing with Cobalt-60 radioactive sources.

Results: Created test documentation that will be used by the Physics Department to conduct formal payload testing and performance evaluation.

Thermal qualification test documentation

What: Contributed to qualification testing activities for aerospace payload systems.

How: Developed and reviewed test procedures based on NASA GEVS requirements, helping define environmental and functional verification tests.

Results: Supported the preparation of qualification testing campaigns and improved understanding of aerospace verification, validation, and environmental testing standards.

Rocketry Club Level 1 Rocket Build

Analysis and simulations

I conducted extensive flight simulations using OpenRocket to evaluate the performance of multiple rocket configurations. Parameters such as fin geometry, mass distribution, and motor selection were varied to assess their impact on apogee, stability margin, velocity, and recovery performance. This process improved my understanding of rocket aerodynamics, design optimisation, and the use of simulation tools to support engineering decision-making.

Fins analysis

Various fin designs were considered. After trying various combinations of shapes, heights, and sweep angles, it was determined that a symmetrical trapezoidal fin design would produce the best aerodynamic results with the best chance of the fin being intact upon landing. Other fin designs that stood out most were the tapered swept and the clipped delta.

Clipped delta wings gave us a stability of 1.56 cal:

Tapered swept wings gave us a stability of 1.66 cal:

After that we did some experimentation with a trapezoidal fin shape. This gave us a stability of 1.53 but also it is important to note that trapezoidal fin shapes are relatively flutter resistant.

Here is a diagram that gives an overview of the different fin designs available:

Each fin has a shoulder extension which is epoxied into slits in the outer tube.

Nose cone

We placed different masses in the nose cone to experiment between stability and apogee. We also experimented with effects of various nose cone shapes on apogee, spanning from ellipsoid to parabolic to conical to finally Haack series, which we used in our final design. A cavity was designed to store an avionics package, sealable from the rest of the build. The nose cone was modelled using CAD software.

Body

The retaining rings and centering rings take on most of the pressure of the motor during burn time. Two stacked centering rings provide support for the engine to push the rocket up. To hold the engine in during the deployment charge detonation, multiple z-clips can be screwed into the centering rings at the very bottom of the rocket to provide retention.

Final product

We had a successful launch and retrieved our rocket in perfect condition. This means that we received our level 1 build certification.

Picosatellite

The main theme for the creation of our payload was retrievability. Our payload's main design was a plume mechanism which served as a visual marker for retrievability. On the firmware side, the payload included a working LoRa, GPS, and sensors. GPS data was transmitted over LoRa during the whole duration of flight. Sensors were continuously polled, and acceleration and altitude data were used to determine the stage of flight. Once landing was detected, the system turned on the plume mechanism in short bursts, stopping to allow cool down.

PCB testing

I took an active role in PCB bring-up and debugging, spending over two months testing, analysing, and troubleshooting our custom avionics boards. I was responsible for flashing and debugging the MSP430 microcontroller, validating hardware-software integration, and supporting multiple iterations of PCB rework and soldering. This involved identifying faults using oscilloscopes and multimeters, implementing fixes, and verifying system performance through repeated testing of the payload and avionics subsystems.

I participated in multiple rounds of PCB rework, including soldering components, repairing connections, and implementing wire modifications to resolve hardware issues and improve reliability. We also carried out repeated functional tests of the vapour-generation payload to ensure consistent operation and mission readiness. This experience gave me significant exposure to PCB bring-up, embedded systems debugging, hardware troubleshooting, and iterative design refinement in a real-project environment.

Testing the coil mechanism

Coils of different sizes were tested to determine the optimal plume output. Through this process, the ideal resistance of the nichrome wire needed to achieve a consistent plume was identified. Volume testing of the plume solution was also conducted to determine the volume required to produce a dense, stable plume. The pluming mechanism consisted of the fan holders, the fan, the plume solution chamber, and the nichrome coil.

Final product

Geothermal Subsidence Modelling — Wairakei

What: Developed a mathematical model to simulate ground subsidence caused by geothermal fluid extraction at the Wairakei reservoir, New Zealand.

How: Built a system of ODEs describing reservoir and mudstone pressure dynamics, implemented numerical solvers in Python, benchmarked against analytical solutions, calibrated model parameters against historical observed data, quantified uncertainty using posterior distributions, and produced stakeholder forecasts for multiple extraction scenarios.

Results: Produced a calibrated, validated model with uncertainty quantification and provided data-driven recommendations to stakeholders on proposed extraction rate changes.

Conceptual model

ODEs for pressure

ODE for subsidence

Subsidence is modelled as a delayed response to the pressure drop in the mudstone relative to its initial state, where positive subsidence indicates a decrease in ground level.

Model benchmark

The numerical solution was benchmarked against an analytical solution to verify correctness. Relative error analysis confirmed errors on the order of 10⁻¹³–10⁻¹⁶, and time step convergence testing demonstrated the solver reaches a stable solution well within acceptable tolerances.

Model calibration for pressure

The model was calibrated by minimising the misfit between simulated and observed pressure data. The ad-hoc guess model showed significant deviation from observations, particularly in the 1960–1980 period. The calibrated model achieved a substantially improved fit across the full historical record, with the pressure misfit reduced and centred around zero.

Best fit to data

Calibration of the subsidence model yielded parameters β = 6.82×10⁻¹ and τ = 4.77, reducing the RMSE from 1.106 to 0.322. The calibrated model closely tracks the observed subsidence curve from 1950 through to 2010, capturing both the rate of onset and the plateau behaviour in later years.

Stakeholder forecasts

Four extraction scenarios were modelled over a 50-year horizon: full shutdown (0 kg/s), current extraction (900 kg/s), slightly increased extraction (1000 kg/s), and the proposed increased rate (1250 kg/s). Higher extraction rates are associated with continued subsidence growth, while shutdown leads to gradual ground level recovery.

Uncertainty

Uncertainty was quantified for the most sensitive model parameters, β and τ, using a posterior probability distribution derived from historical data. The posterior shows a well-constrained region of high probability, indicating the calibrated parameters are robust. Structural errors — such as the lumped reservoir assumption — represent the primary remaining sources of uncertainty.

Recommendation

The group advised against implementing the proposed 1250 kg/s extraction rate. Analysis of the uncertainty fan showed a high probability of differential subsidence exceeding 10 cm/year within the forecast period, which was defined as unacceptable in the project brief. The associated pressure drop under this scenario was also found to be outside acceptable bounds.

Vehicle Routing Problem — Woolworths Delivery Simulation

What: Modelled and simulated Woolworths van delivery routes to evaluate whether introducing a SUB60 vehicle would improve routing efficiency.

How: Used Python to run multiple simulations of weekday and Saturday delivery routes across the Wellington region. Routing configurations were varied and cost outcomes analysed across scenarios. Confidence intervals were applied to compare the average total cost of owned-only fleets versus fleets with a SUB60 van.

Results: Produced cost comparison analysis across both day types, with recommendations supported by statistical confidence intervals from simulation data.

Route simulations

Figure 1 & 2: Routes taken by vans on weekdays and Saturdays. Each colour represents a different vehicle.

Cost comparison

Average total cost comparison between owned-only fleet and fleet with SUB60, across Saturday and weekday routes.

IYPT – International Young Physicists Tournament

The problem:

What: Conducted research into the fluid mechanics of ink-alcohol fractal formation as part of a physics research competition.

How: Experiments were designed and carried out, data was collected and analysed, and mathematical and physical explanations were developed for the observed behaviour. Technical presentations were prepared and findings defended before judges and competing teams.

Results: Developed strong experimental, analytical, and scientific communication skills while successfully presenting complex fluid mechanics concepts in a competitive international-style research environment.

Grapple Christchurch – Engineering Competition

What: Designed and developed an IoT-based flood monitoring and prediction system for an engineering competition.

How: Working within a multidisciplinary team, a sensor network was developed that collected environmental data and combined it with historical flood data to improve prediction capability. Contributed to the CAD design of a compact enclosure mountable on existing infrastructure such as residential mailboxes.

Results: Produced a functional flood-monitoring concept integrating sensors, data analysis, and product design, while gaining experience in IoT systems, CAD modelling, and engineering design for real-world applications.
